claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 10 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 2 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 2 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 8 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 10 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances and the child or children you're asking about.

Let me start with: **Do you have a child or children you're responsible for?** If yes, could you tell me how many children you're asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have children I'm responsible for. I'm asking about three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you assess eligibility for Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

Let me start by asking about their ages. **How old are Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 10, Blake is 19, and Charlie is 2 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Now, regarding Blake who is 19 years old - this is important because Child Benefit generally stops when a child turns 16, unless they're in approved education or training.

**Is Blake currently in approved education or training?** (For example, full-time non-advanced education like A-levels, Scottish Highers, or approved vocational training)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in education or training at the moment. They finished school a while back and aren't studying anything right now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that about Blake.

Now let me ask about you and the children: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to ask about your relationship with each child:

**Are you the parent of Alex, Blake, and Charlie, or do you have a different relationship with them?** (For example, are you their grandparent, foster carer, or another type of guardian?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm their parent.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to ask about the living arrangements:

**Do Alex, Blake, and Charlie all live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex and Blake live with me at home, but Charlie doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to understand Charlie's situation a bit more.

**Where does Charlie live?** (For example, does Charlie live with another parent, a relative, in care, or somewhere else?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Charlie lives with their other parent - my ex-partner.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that. This is an important situation because when a child lives with someone else, generally only one person can claim Child Benefit for that child.

**Is your ex-partner currently claiming Child Benefit for Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not sure if they're claiming it or not, to be honest.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's okay. Let me ask a related question: **Do you contribute to the costs of raising Charlie?** (For example, do you provide regular financial support, pay for their clothing, food, or other expenses?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not contributing financially at the moment - I haven't been able to afford to help out with any costs for Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for being honest about that. I have a few more questions to make sure I have a complete picture.

**Regarding Alex and Blake who live with you - are either of them currently in hospital or in local authority care for an extended period?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in care for a little while - just a couple of months or so. But Blake hasn't been in care or hospital.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know about Alex. This is important because there are specific rules about Child Benefit when a child is in local authority care.

**How long has Alex been in care - can you tell me more precisely? Is it less than 8 weeks, between 8-12 weeks, or more than 12 weeks?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been less than 8 weeks - about 7 weeks actually.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

**Are you still contributing to Alex's costs even though they're in care?** (For example, are you providing money, clothing, or other support for Alex?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am - I still help out with their needs and buy things for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know, thank you.

One final question: **Are there any other circumstances I should know about regarding you or the children?** For example:
- Are any of the children currently in hospital for an extended period?
- Do you receive any income or have a partner with income over £50,000 (which could affect the High Income Child Benefit Charge, though not eligibility itself)?
- Any other special circumstances?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, actually - Charlie has been in hospital for a while now, about a couple of months. They've been in the ward getting treatment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that important information about Charlie. This could affect the situation.

**Do you contribute to any of Charlie's costs, even while they're in hospital?** (For example, do you provide money, buy clothing, toiletries, or other items for Charlie?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to afford to spend money on their needs while they're in there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for answering all my questions. Let me now assess the eligibility for Child Benefit for each of your three children based on the information you've provided.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

Based on the information you've provided, here's my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

### **Alex (10 years old): ✅ ELIGIBLE**
You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex has been in local authority care for about 7 weeks, you continue to contribute to their costs. The rules allow Child Benefit to continue for up to 12 weeks when a child is in care, as long as you're still contributing to their costs.

### **Blake (19 years old): ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE**
You are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. Child Benefit stops when a child turns 16 unless they're in approved education or training (which can extend eligibility up to age 20). Since Blake is 19 and not currently in education or training, you cannot claim Child Benefit for them.

### **Charlie (2 years old): ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE**
You are not eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie. Even though Charlie is under 16, they don't live with you and you don't contribute to their costs. When a child lives with someone else and you don't contribute financially, only the person the child lives with (or who contributes to their costs) can claim Child Benefit. Your ex-partner would be the one eligible to claim for Charlie.

---

**In summary:** You can claim Child Benefit for Alex only. If you have any questions about this assessment or need to know how to make a claim, please let me know!

reasonings: Alex is 10 years old (under 16), lives with you, and you are their parent. Although Alex has been in local authority care for approximately 7 weeks (less than 8 weeks), you continue to contribute to their costs. Under the rules, Child Benefit can continue when a child is in care for up to 12 weeks if you continue to contribute to their costs. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 19 years old and is not in approved education or training. Child Benefit stops when a child turns 16 unless they are in approved education or training (up to age 20). Since Blake has finished school and is not currently studying or in approved training, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 2 years old (under 16) but does not live with you - they live with your ex-partner. Charlie has been in hospital for approximately 2 months. You do not contribute financially to Charlie's costs. When a child doesn't live with you and you don't contribute to their costs, you cannot claim Child Benefit for that child. The person who has the child living with them (or who contributes to their costs) would be the one eligible to claim. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a UK resident parent of three children. They are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex (10 years old) who lives with them and for whom they continue to contribute costs despite a short period in care (under 8 weeks). They are not eligible for Blake (19, not in education/training) as Child Benefit ends at 16 unless in approved education. They are not eligible for Charlie (2 years old) who lives with the other parent and for whom the claimant does not contribute financially.

Blake is 19 years old and is not in approved education or training. Child Benefit stops when a child turns 16 unless they are in approved education or training (up to age 20). Since Blake has finished school and is not currently studying or in approved training, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 2 years old (under 16) but does not live with you - they live with your ex-partner. Charlie has been in hospital for approximately 2 months. You do not contribute financially to Charlie's costs. When a child doesn't live with you and you don't contribute to their costs, you cannot claim Child Benefit for that child. The person who has the child living with them (or who contributes to their costs) would be the one eligible to claim. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a UK resident parent of three children. They are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex (10 years old) who lives with them and for whom they continue to contribute costs despite a short period in care (under 8 weeks). They are not eligible for Blake (19, not in education/training) as Child Benefit ends at 16 unless in approved education. They are not eligible for Charlie (2 years old) who lives with the other parent and for whom the claimant does not contribute financially.
